coredata.dc:title: 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite material modified by CQDs with up-conversion characteristics: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation and photolysis of water for hydrogen production

coredata.dc:description: Semiconductor photocatalysis is considered as an effective technology to ease the global energy shortage, designing efficient photocatalysts with enhanced light absorption, carrier separation and surface reaction is still one of the main strategies to solve its practical application. In this paper, the structure of the synthesized material was controlled by regular polystyrene (PS) colloidal spheres as templates, and the 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite modified by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with three-dimensional ordered macroporous structure was prepared. In this composite, CQDs with up-conversion characteristics are used as photosensitizers to expand light absorption. The combination of SrTiO3 and TiO2 enhances the separation efficiency of charges, and the three-dimensional ordered macropore structure reduces the resistance of substances entering the catalyst, further improving the catalytic performance of the material. Under the irradiation of xenon lamp, the hydrogen evolution of the prepared CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite reached 578.95 μmol·g−1 after 8 h of photolysis, which is 14.5 times that of the monomer TiO2. In addition, with Rhodamine B (RhB) as the model molecule, the photocatalytic degradation activity of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 was significantly better than that of other contrast systems under the experimental conditions of multimode photocatalytic degradation, showing enhanced photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production from water photolysis. This study provides a feasible method for preparing three-dimensional ordered macroporous materials with excellent photocatalytic properties.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Li Li, FemaleD., Professor, Doctoral tutor, mainly engaged in the preparation and photocatalytic properties of nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is considered as an effective technology to ease the global energy shortage, designing efficient photocatalysts with enhanced light absorption, carrier separation and surface reaction is still one of the main strategies to solve its practical application. In this paper, the structure of the synthesized material was controlled by regular polystyrene (PS) colloidal spheres as templates, and the 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite modified by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with three-dimensional ordered macroporous structure was prepared. In this composite, CQDs with up-conversion characteristics are used as photosensitizers to expand light absorption. The combination of SrTiO3 and TiO2 enhances the separation efficiency of charges, and the three-dimensional ordered macropore structure reduces the resistance of substances entering the catalyst, further improving the catalytic performance of the material. Under the irradiation of xenon lamp, the hydrogen evolution of the prepared CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite reached 578.95 μmol·g−1 after 8 h of photolysis, which is 14.5 times that of the monomer TiO2. In addition, with Rhodamine B (RhB) as the model molecule, the photocatalytic degradation activity of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 was significantly better than that of other contrast systems under the experimental conditions of multimode photocatalytic degradation, showing enhanced photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production from water photolysis. This study provides a feasible method for preparing three-dimensional ordered macroporous materials with excellent photocatalytic properties.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In this composite, CQDs with up-conversion characteristics are used as photosensitizers to expand light absorption. The combination of SrTiO3 and TiO2 enhances the separation efficiency of charges, and the three-dimensional ordered macropore structure reduces the resistance of substances entering the catalyst, further improving the catalytic performance of the material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is one of the most common photocatalytic materials, which has obvious photocatalytic activity, high chemical stability, strong redox capacity, low cost, and non-toxicity, so it has been widely studied in the field of photocatalysis [1–5]. However, it is also well known that monomer TiO2 has low utilization rate of sunlight. In the past decades, people have been adopting many new methods, such as metal and nonmetal doping, dye sensitization, semiconductor combination, microstructure engineering, etc [6–10], to improve the photocatalytic ability of titanium dioxide. Among them, the formation of heterostructure through different materials compositing is considered as one of the most effective methods. The synthesized catalyst has the advantages of all components, and their synergistic effect can reduce the disadvantages from the structure and function of a single component [11–13]. It is worth noting that the photocatalytic ability can be different due to the selection of diverse catalysts loading. Although some materials have heterostructure after compositing, the catalytic performance and stability are also unsatisfactory. For example, in the case of CdS/TiO2 and WO3/TiO2 [14,15], the photodegradation progress of the composite catalyst is small and its stability is lower than that of the monomer TiO2. Therefore, it is crucial to find a matched material with good stability as the second semiconductor.

Strontium perovskite titanate (SrTiO3) is a new semiconductor with a band gap of 3.4 eV. Because of its high conduction potential, it has attracted more attention in the field of hydrolysis and organic mineralization [16–19]. Some studies have shown that strontium titanate has excellent physical and chemical stability, structural flexibility and photoinduced corrosion, and thus it is a preferred material for hydrogen catalysis [20,21]. However, the band gap of SrTiO3 is still not suitable for absorbing the whole visible light, which means almost half of the visible light cannot be utilized. Therefore, the hydrogen evolution efficiency of SrTiO3 is limited by solar energy to some extent. Obviously, the combination of SrTiO3 and other semiconductors, the modification with photosensitizers, and the change of structure and morphology for forming nanocomposites will be effective ways to solve this problem.

The three-dimensional layered pore framework of three-dimensional ordered macroporous materials can significantly improve the mass transfer of solution through the framework, and ensure high surface area and sufficient redox reaction active sites [22]. In recent years, the preparation of three-dimensional ordered structural materials has made some gratifying achievements. Due to the periodic distribution of the dielectric constant, three-dimensional ordered macroporous materials have good permeability of macroporous channels, which can promote the rapid transport of substances in the system during the reaction process. In addition, some researchers believe that the materials with this morphology can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron holes in the reaction process, thus improving the photocatalytic activity [23–28].

CQDs are a class of attractive nanostructured carbon. Their preparation methods are simple and cheap, and they have some very ideal properties, such as ultra-small size, good biocompatibility, high chemical inertia, adjustable hydrophilicity, rich surface functional groups and antifouling properties [29–33]. Therefore, CQDs have obvious advantages: efficient solar absorption, tunable photoluminescence (PL), infrared response up-conversion photoluminescence and unique photo-induced electron transfer characteristics. In the research, people can change the surface functional groups of CQDs by selecting a specific synthesis route to achieve tunable photoluminescence conversion, and then use it for different purposes in optical research. It is noteworthy that based on the up-conversion characteristics of CQDs, a series of photocatalyst composites with visible light response are prepared, which is expected to greatly improve the utilization of sunlight for photocatalysts.

Based on this, a simple and feasible method was proposed in this study to successfully prepare the three-dimensional ordered structure composite material CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. The design idea of the whole research is as follows: first, the PS colloidal sphere is used as the macroporous template, and TiO2 and SrTiO3 are compounded by the sol–gel method. After recombination, 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 forms a heterostructure due to the difference in conduction band and valence band between SrTiO3 and TiO2 and the difference in semiconductor structure, thus providing a new path for the transfer of electron–hole pairs. Then, CQDs were modified on 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites. Because of the unique up-conversion characteristics of CQDs, the utilization of light by CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 could be improved, and at the same time, it could provide a new transfer path for electron–hole, reduce the coincidence rate of electron–hole, so as to improve the activity of the composite. Finally, the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 was studied from two aspects including photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and photolysis of water to produce hydrogen.

Compared with previous reported methods, this research has the following advantages: (1) The synthetic material has an open and transparent three-dimensional ordered macroporous structure, which provides sufficient space for the flow of solution molecules in the reaction system, and provides rich active sites for photochemical redox reactions; (2) The proper combination of SrTiO3 and TiO2 improves the effective separation of photogenerated carriers; 3) The up-conversion property of CQDs in the composite material converts the long-wavelength light into the short-wavelength light, which broadens the absorption range to light.
See the Supplementary material for a detailed experimental procedure.

In order to study the crystal structure of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, 3DOM TiO2, 3DOM SrTiO3, 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, and CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 were analyzed by X-ray diffraction, and the results are shown in Fig. 1.

It can be seen that after calcination at 650 ℃, the diffraction peaks of 3DOM TiO2 are mainly located at 25.06°, 37.48° and 47.56°, which match well with the crystal planes of (101), (004) and (200), respectively, indicating that it is anatase crystal phase (JCPDS 21–1272) [34]. The diffraction peaks of 3DOM SrTiO3 are located at 22.78°, 32.42°, 39.98°, 46.48°, 57.79°, 67.80° and 77.17°, corresponding to (100), (110), (111), (200), (211), (220) and (310) crystal planes (JCPDS 35–0734) [35], respectively. For 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites, the diffraction peaks of both TiO2 and SrTiO3 can be found in the diffraction peaks of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 and the diffraction peaks of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 are similar to that of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. Moreover, the diffraction peaks at 32.42° and 39.98° in CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 correspond to (110) and (111) crystal planes in SrTiO3. Above results show that the crystal forms of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 and 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 mainly exist as TiO2 anatase. It is noteworthy that no diffraction peak of CQDs is found in CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, which is due to the low loading of CQDs in the composite [36]. To verify the existence of CQDs, FT-IR and Raman tests were conducted on CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. FT-IR spectra of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 given in Fig.S1(a) show the peak at 3420 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O-H and bending vibrations of adsorbed water molecules, indicating the existence of O-H on the surface of TiO2. The strong broad absorption peak in the range of 3100–3450 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H stretching vibrations of SrTiO3-TiO2 composite, which suggests that two different semiconductors interact with each other during treatment, resulting in the existence of more O-H on the surface of composite photocatalysts. At the same time, the absorption peak near 1400 cm−1 is due to the Ti-O-Ti vibration, which further reveals two different semiconductors interact after combination[37]. Moreover, the characteristic peak of TiO6 (607 cm−1) is also observed [38]. The peak at 1638 cm−1 belongs to the CC stretching vibration of sp2 hybridization, which is the most critical functional group and a characteristic marker in CQDs. The absorption peak at 1076 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the C–O bond. The peak at 1635 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of CO [39,40], which further proves the presence of CQDs in composite materials. Fig.S1(b) shows the Raman spectrum of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. The main Raman bands are located at 144, 195, 395, 515 and 639 cm−1, respectively, corresponding to Eg(1), Eg(2), B1 g, A1 g, and Eg(3) of anatase. No Raman shifts related to vibrational modes of SrTiO3 are detected because a high degree of symmetry exists in the cubic perovskite structure, and thus resulting in no representative signal [41].

In order to verify the crystallite size of the CQDs synthesized in this paper, CQDs were also analyzed by X-ray diffraction method as shown in Fig.S2. No specific crystal phase is observed in Fig.S2, but the strong diffraction peak centered at 2θ= 27° shows that CQDs originate from glucose, which has been widely recognized [42,43].

The crystallite size of each sample was calculated by Jade software, which is based on the Scherrer's formula (see Formula S1), and the results are shown in Table S1. It can be seen from Table S1 that the crystallite size of all photocatalysts is about 12–16 nm, and the average crystal size of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 modified by CQDs is larger than that of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, which indicates that the loading of CQDs affects the crystal size of composites to some extent.

The light absorption performances of 3DOM TiO2, 3DOM SrTiO3, 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 were analyzed by UV–visible diffuse reflectance absorption spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2. It can be seen from Fig. 2(a) that 3DOM TiO2 and 3DOM SrTiO3 are consistent with previous reports [44,45]. It is worth noting that compared with the composite without CQDs modification, CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 shows stronger absorption capacity in the visible region, and the UV–visible absorption curve of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 moves to a longer wavelength, with a red shift to nearly 600 nm. This result can be attributed to the unique absorption performance of CQDs and the up-conversion characteristics of CQDs [46].

In addition, according to Fig. 2(b) and Kubelka–Munk formula (see Formula S2) [36], the band gap energies of 3DOM TiO2, 3DOM SrTiO3, 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 were calculated, and the obtained values are shown in Table S1. The Eg value of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is obviously lower than that of 3DOM SrTiO2, 3DOM SrTiO3 and 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, which indicates that the three-dimensional ordered macroporous composite CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has higher photocatalytic activity to some extent.

The chemical bond and surface chemical state of CQDS/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite was further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3(a), the spectrum shows that there are Ti, O, Sr and C in the sample. Fig. 3(b) is the XPS spectra of Ti in the binding energy region of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 in the photocatalytic material CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. The result shows Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 have the binding energies of 457.1 eV and 463.27 eV, respectively, which is the characteristic peak of TiO2 [47–49]. Fig. 3(c) shows the binding energies of O 1 s with 530.2 eV and 532.2 eV, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of lattice oxygen and hydroxyl oxygen [50]. Fig. 3(d) is the XPS spectra of Sr 3d, with binding energies of 132.8 eV, 133.5 eV and 134.6 eV, which proves that the element exists in the form of Sr2+ ion [51,52]. Fig. 3(e) shows the XPS spectra of C in the C 1 s binding energy region, where the peak of 283.7 eV is attributed to the external carbon source, and the binding energies of the other peaks are 284.3 eV, 285.3 eV and 288.2 eV, of which 284.3 eV corresponds to the CC bond, and 285.3 eV and 288.2 eV correspond to C-O and CO [36], respectively, proving the existence of CQDs.

At the same time, XPS tests were conducted on TiO2, SrTiO3, SrTiO3-TiO2 and CQDs. Fig.S3(a, d, h, l) show the full spectra of TiO2, SrTiO3, SrTiO3-TiO2 and CQDs. The XPS spectra of Ti in Fig.S3(b, e, i) demonstrate the binding energies of Ti at 457.1 eV and 463.27 eV, indicating that Ti exists in the form of Ti4+ in the composite [47–49]. Fig.S3(c, f, j) show the XPS spectra of O 1 s and reveal that O exists in the form of lattice oxygen and hydroxyl oxygen[50]. Fig.S3(g, k) show the XPS spectra of Sr 3d, suggesting that Sr exists in the form of Sr2+ ion [51,52]. However, compared with pure SrTiO3, SrTiO3-TiO2 has obvious changes in the electronic binding energy of Sr element in the composite, which can be attributed to the introduction of other components [53]. From Fig. S3(m), we can see that CC, C-O and CO present in CQDs, this is consistent with the bond energy of CQDs in composite materials. It is worth noting that CC is the most critical functional group in CQDs and a characteristic marker of their formation [54].

The morphology of prepared CQDS/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 sample was characterized by scanning electron microscopy. Fig. 4(a) and (b) shows the SEM photos of PS colloidal spheres with different magnification. It can be seen that the synthesized PS colloidal spheres are uniform in size, monodisperse, closely packed in structure, and orderly arranged in three-dimensional space, with an average diameter of about 300 nm. Fig. 4(c, d) shows that CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has interpenetrating macropores and uniform pore size, with an average pore size of about 200 nm. Its periodic network structure in three-dimensional space ensures the smooth entry of substances into the macropore, reduces the resistance of substances in the diffusion process, thus greatly improves the efficiency of substances in the transport and diffusion process, and promotes the catalytic performance [51]. When light enters the 3DOM structure, strong scattering occurs, and the scattered light can be reabsorbed. At the same time, it can ensure the reflectivity at different incident light angles, and reduce the influence of random orientation in the photocatalytic reaction process during continuous stirring of the solution. It can be seen from Fig. 4 that the pore size of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is significantly smaller than the diameter of PS colloidal spheres, which is due to the shrinkage of the pore size of PS colloidal spheres during the calcination process. Through calculation, the pore shrinkage of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 can reach 33 %.

Fig.S4 shows SEM images of TiO2, SrTiO3, SrTiO3-TiO2, 3DOM TiO2, 3DOM SrTiO3 and 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. TiO2, SrTiO3 and SrTiO3-TiO2 without PS microspheres are irregular in shape, whereas 3DOM SrTiO3 and 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 prepared with PS microspheres have three-dimensional ordered macroporous structure. The EDS mapping spectra of composite material CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is shown in Fig. 5. Obviously, there are four elements in the composite material: Ti, Sr, O and C, and the four elements are uniformly distributed. The result of C confirms that CQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2; The EDS results of Sr and Ti further show the combination of SrTiO3 and TiO2. In addition, from the above EDS images, it can be observed that CQDs, SrTiO3 and TiO2 in the CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite are in close contact, which further indicates that the successful modification of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 by CQDs is conducive to the separation and transfer of electron–hole pairs, thus improving the photocatalytic performance of the composite.

In addition, in order to obtain more information about the skeleton of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites, TEM analysis was carried out on the sample, and the analysis results are shown in Fig. 6. The black part in the TEM image is the skeleton structure of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, and the bright part corresponds to the macropore. It can be seen from Fig. 6 that the CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 macroporous structure is uniform and regularly connected. Furthermore, through TEM photos, it is not difficult to find that CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 exists as a multi-layer network structure, which is due to close accumulation of PS spheres between self-assembled layers after calcination. TEM results further prove that CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 exists in the form of periodic reticulated multilayer macroporous structure, which is consistent with the analysis results of SEM. Fig.S5 demonstrates TEM images of TiO2, SrTiO3, SrTiO3-TiO2 and CQDs. As shown in Fig.S5(a–c), TiO2, SrTiO3 and SrTiO3-TiO2 are irregular without a three-dimensional ordered structure. By combining with Fig. 6, it can be seen that PS colloidal sphere changes the morphology of the material, resulting in a better spatial geometric structure in space. Fig.S5(d) reveals CQDs have good dispersion with a diameter of approximately 2 nm in size, further confirming the successful synthesis of CQDs.

Fig. 7(a, b) show the HR-TEM images of three-dimensional order CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the composite has a transparent macroporous structure, which is consistent with the results of SEM. It can be seen that small nanoparticles of CQDs are present in the composite, which is consistent with TEM images in Fig.S5(d). Through further analysis as shown in Fig. 7(c–h), it is not difficult to find that these nanoparticles have obvious lattice fringes. The lattice stripes were estimated by Gatan digital microscope software, and the calculated lattice spacing is about 0.321 nm, which is related to the lattice spacing of CQDs [31]. It can be seen from Fig. 7(b) that the CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites have lattice stripes of carbon quantum dots, titanium dioxide and strontium titanate, indicating that the three-dimensional ordered CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites were successfully synthesized. At the same time, we can observe the lattice stripes estimated by Gatan digital microscope software of SrTiO3(110) and TiO2(101) as shown in Fig. 7(d, h), displaying the lattice spacing of SrTiO3(110) is 0.27 nm [20], and the lattice spacing of TiO2(101) is 0.35 nm [34]. These results are consistent with the results of XRD analysis. In addition, the overlapping of lattice stripe planes of carbon quantum dots, titanium dioxide and strontium titanate indicates that there are strong bonding effects among three components.

The above results show that the pore wall of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites, consisting of CQDs, SrTiO3 and TiO2 particles, not only retains the original characteristics of nanoparticles, but also shows the excellent properties of macroporous materials. The morphology of the composite is very conducive to the separation and transfer of electron–hole pairs, which is expected to improve the photocatalytic performance of the composite.
The surface physical and chemical properties of different samples were tested by N2 adsorption–desorption tests. The results are shown in Fig. 8 and Table S2.

It can be seen from Fig. 8 that the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of all materials belong to the type II adsorption curve, which is a typical mesoporous structure. The source of this mesoporous structure should be the accumulation of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 on the macroporous wall. According to IUPAC definition, the hysteresis loop of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 belongs to H1 hysteresis loop, indicating that the composite material has relatively large pore size and good pore connectivity [52].

From the data in Table S2, the specific surface area (BET) of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is larger than that of 3DOM SrTiO3 and 3DOM TiO2, which is due to the smaller average pore size of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. Compared with 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has smaller BET, which probably because the modification of CQDs blocks the macropores, resulting in the reduction of BET. Large pore volume, specific surface area and high porosity can further improve the transmission and diffusion ability of electron–hole pairs, and increase the adsorption capacity of reactants on the surface of photocatalyst, thus making full use of the active sites in the catalyst.

In order to further study the separation efficiency of photoelectron–hole pairs, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) analysis was carried out at room temperature. In general, PL spectrum is helpful to analyze the migration and transfer of electrons because the overall strength of PL spectrum depends on the recombination process of electron–hole pairs. Higher overall intensity in PL spectra leads to greater recombination rate of electron–hole pairs [37,38,49,53]. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst can be analyzed by PL spectra. At the same time, PL spectrum analysis is used to determine whether the prepared CQDs have up-conversion characteristics. As shown in Fig.S6, the CQDs are excited with light at 660–780 nm wavelength, and then CQDs convert it into light at 350–550 nm, which proves that the prepared CQDs have up-conversion characteristics. It can be seen from Fig.S6 that the light absorption band edge of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is at 580 nm, so the emitted light after the conversion of CQDs can be well utilized by CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. Therefore, the use of CQDs can increase the utilization of light for the catalyst and expand it to the visible light region.

In order to study the migration, transfer and recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs in the prepared composites, PL spectra of the prepared composites were also tested and results of the monomer TiO2, 3DOM TiO2, 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, CQD/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites are shown in Fig. 9. The monomer TiO2 exhibits the highest absorption peaks at 452 nm and 470 nm in the photoluminescence spectrum [55], and other samples have similar trends with the monomer TiO2. It is noteworthy that the PL intensity of 3DOM TiO2 is significantly lower than that of monomer TiO2, indicating that the existence of 3DOM structure can improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges. In addition, the PL intensity of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is lower than that of 3DOM TiO2, which means that the combination of SrTiO3 and TiO2 can promote the transfer of photogenerated charges to a certain extent. Furthermore, CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has the lowest PL intensity, indicating that CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 can further improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges, which is due to the synergy of CQDs, SrTiO3 and TiO2, as well as the close contact of the three components. Therefore, these results show that the preparation of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 can enhance light absorption and accelerate the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers in the system.

In order to intuitively see the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers in photocatalysts, transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out. It is commonly recognized that the charge separation efficiency of the catalyst is proportionate to the photocurrent density. It can be seen from Fig. 10(a) that TiO2 shows the lowest photocurrent response because of the rapidly reconstituted monomer of photogenerated carriers. The composite material CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 shows the highest photocurrent intensity, indicating that it has excellent photo-induced charge separation and rapid electron transport, which may be due to the advantages of 3DOM structure and the broadening absorption spectrum area caused by the synergistic effect of CQDs and SrTiO3.

As shown in Fig. 10(b), compared with monomer TiO2, 3DOM TiO2 has a smaller impedance arc radius, which indicates that the electron transfer resistance is lower. It is important to mention that the impedance arc radius of the composite material CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is the smallest, which means that the charge transfer resistance of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is the smallest, indicating it has the best electron transfer ability and charge transfer rate. The photoelectrochemical properties of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 show that the homogeneous wall thickness and the synergistic effect of each component can significantly promote the charge transfer and separation in the composite. The model of equivalent circuit is shown in Fig. 10(b).

Adsorption properties of different catalysts were performed. As shown in Fig.S7, adsorption of all catalysts is less than 10%, which rules out the possibility that the degradation of dyes by the catalysts is caused by adsorption. In order to determine the effect of the content of CQDs, SrTiO3 and TiO2 on photocatalytic activity, photocatalytic degradation experiments of RhB were conducted on different proportions of photocatalysts under UV light irradiation. As shown in Fig.S8, the optimal degradation effect is achieved when the molar ratio of Sr to Ti is 0.4 and the addition volume of CQDs is 1 mL.

In order to investigate the photocatalytic performance of composite material CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2, the photodegradation experiments were carried out with RhB as the model molecule under three different photocatalytic conditions of ultraviolet light, visible light and simulated sunlight. The relevant results are shown in Fig. 11 and Fig.S9. It can be seen from Fig. 11(a) that the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is significantly higher than that of other systems when the RhB is degraded by ultraviolet photocatalysis.

In Fig. 11(b), the relationship between -lnC
0
/C
t and reaction time t is basically linear, which shows that the degradation of RhB basically follows quasi-first order reaction kinetics [56]. It can be seen from Fig. 11(c) and Fig.S9 that under simulated and visible light irradiation, CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 shows excellent photocatalytic degradation of RhB, proving that the photocatalytic activity of 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 is significantly improved after modified by CQDs. Therefore, the up-conversion characteristics of CQDs helps to improve the utilization rate of light in the composite materials. Fig.S10 shows the experimental results of the ultraviolet degradation of different dyes by CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites. It can be seen that CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has certain degradation effects on other dyes and salicylic acid. It is obvious that the photocatalytic degradation of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has certain universality. Fig. 11(d) shows the results of the ultraviolet capture experiments of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites to investigate the main active species of the composite in the process of photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 11(d), after adding hole trapping agent triethanolamine (TEA) and superoxide radical trapping agent p-benzoquinone (BQ), the degradation rate of ultraviolet photodegradation on RhB decreases significantly, indicating that the hole (h+) and superoxide radical (·O2-) are the main active factors in the photocatalytic process of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. However, the degradation efficiency only has a small change after adding tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), which proves that hydroxyl radical (·OH) only plays an auxiliary role in the photocatalytic process.

Fig. 12(a) shows the experimental results of hydrogen evolution of different catalysts. It can be seen that after 8 h of xenon lamp irradiation, CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has the best performance of hydrogen production by photolysis of water, and the amount of hydrogen evolution reaches 578.95 μmol·g−1, which is 14.5 times of 3DOM TiO2. The reason for this may be that CQDs loading improves the light utilization of the composite. At the same time, the composite is a three-dimensional ordered macroporous material, which can not only provide more active sites, but also increase the refractive index of light, resulting in longer dwell time of light staying in CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2. In addition, compared with other research results (Table S4), CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 still performs well photocatalytic performance.

In order to investigate the stability of the catalyst, cycle experiments were carried out, and the results are shown in Fig. 12(b). After three cycles, the hydrogen evolution of the composite material CQDS/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 can still reach 447.65 μmol·g−1, indicating that the composite material has good stability. However, after three cycles, the hydrogen production of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 decreases by a small amount, which is due to reducing light utilization of the composite caused by the shedding of some CQDs. PL and UV–vis/DRS spectra analysis was further performed on the catalyst after cyclic testing. From Fig.S11(a), the PL intensity of CQD/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has an increase after cycling, indicating that its electron transfer rate becomes weak and the recombination ability of electron–hole pairs increases, resulting in the reduction of photocatalytic ability. From Fig.S11(b), UV–vis absorption curve of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 after cycling shows a blue shift, indicating a decrease in its utilization of sunlight.

In order to further evaluate the changes in the composite material after cycling tests, XRD and XPS analysis was conducted as shown in Fig.S12. Fig.S12(a) shows diffraction peaks at 25.06°, 37.48° and 47.56°, corresponding to the crystal planes of (101), (004) and (200), respectively, which match with anatase crystal phase (JCPDS 21–1272). Moreover, the diffraction peaks at 32.42° and 39.98° correspond to the crystal planes of (110) and (111) crystal planes in SrTiO3, indicating that the composite material has good stability and the crystal phase does not change. From Fig.S12(b), there are still four elements Ti, O, Sr, and C in the composite material. Moreover, Ti, Sr, and C are also unchanged as shown in Fig.S12(c, e, f). It is worth noting that the XPS spectra of the O element given in Fig.S12(d) shows a particularly large shift with a broad peak appearing at 535 eV. This may be due to the addition of Na2S&Na2SO3 as sacrificial agents during the photolysis of water, where the combination of Na and O contributes to a sodium Auger peak (Na KLL)[57–59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 photocatalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results and relevant experimental data, we speculate that CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 has two possible photogenerated carrier transmission paths, as shown in Fig. 13. As we discussed earlier, under the irradiation of light, the three-dimensional ordered macroporous structure can increase the light absorption, and CQDs have the up-conversion property, which can convert the light of long wavelength into the light of short wavelength to broaden the light response range of the composite, thus greatly improving the utilization rate of the catalyst to light.

It is well known that the experiment of hydrogen production by photolysis of water needs to meet the following conditions: (1) the conduction band position of semiconductor should be negative to the reaction potential of hydrogen electrode (0 eV), so that the photoelectron energy can meet the requirements of hydrogen evolution reaction; (2) The valence band position should be positive with the reaction potential of the oxygen electrode (1.23 eV), so that the photogenerated hole can effectively oxidize water [60]. The calculation formula of semiconductor ECB and EVB can be found in the Supporting information (see Formula S3), and the results are shown in Table S3.

According to the results of the CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite capture experiments, it is not difficult to find that the main active species participating in the photocatalytic reaction are·O2 – and h+, while the content of·OH is very small, which indicates that a small part of the valence band accumulated by h+ is oxidized by hydroxyl or water molecules adsorbed on the surface to produce·OH. Fig. 13(a) shows the traditional photocatalysis mechanism: under simulated sunlight or ultraviolet light, each component in the composite is activated to produce electron–hole pairs. Photoinduced electrons are transferred from the conduction band (CB) of SrTiO3 to the CB of TiO2 and then collected by CQDs. The photogenerated electrons in CQDs react with the dissolved oxygen on the surface of catalyst to form strong oxidizing·O2 –, which can degrade pollutants into CO2 and H2O. At the same time, photogenerated holes accumulates on the valence band (VB) of SrTiO3, which can directly degrade RhB or react with water molecules adsorbed on the catalyst surface to generate·OH, and thus further degrading RhB.

It is well known that a more negative valence band means that it is easier to produce superoxide radicals. Therefore, in combination with the previous analysis, we prefer another possible mechanism, which is shown in Fig. 13(b). First, with the help of up-conversion characteristics of CQDs, long-wavelength light is converted into short-wavelength light, in which the latter can be absorbed and utilized by the catalyst. Second, CQDs act as the electron transfer medium to accelerate the electron transfer rate. At the same time, the electron–hole pair plays a "bridge" role in the recombination, which constructs a unique Z-shaped structure, prolongs the transfer path of photogenerated carriers, and thus improves the efficiency of photocatalytic reaction. Moreover, CQDs play the role of electronic receivers, receiving electrons on the CB of TiO2, thus extending the life of electrons. The electrons accumulated on the CQDs react with dissolved oxygen in water to generate·O2 –, and react with dye molecules to generate CO2 and H2O. Some of the holes on the VB of TiO2 directly degrade RhB, and the other part reacts with water to generate·OH and then degrade RhB.

In this study, 3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composites modified by CQDs with up-conversion properties were successfully prepared using PS colloidal spheres as templates. CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3-TiO2 composite materials utilize up-conversion characteristics in CQDs, which converts long-wavelength light into short-wavelength light to increase the utilization rate of composite materials in the visible light region. CQDs also act as electronic receivers, providing a new path for electron transfer and forming a "Z-bridge" to accelerate the transfer of electron pairs and suppress the recombination rate electron–hole pairs. At the same time, the unique 3DOM structure further improves the light absorption performance of the composite material and enhances its light absorption efficiency. The synergy of CQDs, SrTiO3 and TiO2 prolongs the separation time of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, and thus the photocatalytic performance of the prepared composites is further improved.
